misconception: All wines improve with age if stored correctly.
why_incorrect: The vast majority of wines are made to be consumed young, within 1–3 years of production. Wines that improve with age are structurally specific: they require sufficient tannin (reds), acidity, residual sugar (some whites), or specific compounds (Sauternes, Sherry) that evolve favourably over time. A simple, fresh Pinot Grigio or Beaujolais Nouveau will not improve with extended cellaring — it will lose its primary fruit character and gain no complexity. The ability to age is a stylistic quality of certain wines, not a universal property. On the SAT, readiness to drink and ageing potential are assessed as part of quality, not as a default assumption.
corrected_understanding: Only wines with specific structural characteristics — high tannin, high acidity, residual sugar, or particular stylistic qualities — benefit from extended ageing. Most wines are made to be consumed young.
detection_signals: doesn't any wine get better if you store it long enough
if you keep a wine longer it will always improve
this wine must be better because it's been aged for ten years
I said it had high ageing potential because all wines improve with time
